1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is unambiguous: correct_answer letters from GPQA Diamond plus empirical difficulty; scoring is deterministic with calibrated reward tables, SDT statistics, and entropy-vs-difficulty calibration. Robust backward-iterating JSON parsing with letter fallbacks handles edge cases (t01, t04, t11).

2_construct_validity.justification: Primary evals send GPQA prompts to a real model (gpt-oss-120b) and score its responses (e.g. ANSWER/DELEGATE behavior, neutral-vs-affective paired framing, signal/distractor binary pairs). Paired conditions deliberately isolate behavioral metacognition from confounds; tightly aligned with the stated metacognition construct.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean modular code: _safe_prompt wraps calls with graceful failure, dedicated parsers with fallbacks, per-question CSV logging, completion-rate handling, extensive docstrings. Uses kbench.task idiomatically across 11 notebooks.

4_sample_size.justification: Each task merges to 80 GPQA Diamond questions (>20); t11 runs 160 prompts. Datasets confirm 80-row task files. Well above threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple distinct dimensions: t01 reward + delegation AUC, t04 confidence entropy calibration vs empirical difficulty, t11 type-2 SDT (d-hat, d*, MC ratio), t06 confidence-shift + answer-flip composite. Choices justified in markdown/docstrings with references.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook header articulates the exact capability (behavioral vs declarative metacognition), why it matters, distinctions between sub-tasks (ER disrupts vs PI adjusts), and ties to experimental Aims with literature references (Wang 2025, Maniscalco & Lau 2012).

7_technical_documentation.justification: Comprehensive docstrings describe dataset provenance (GPQA Diamond, 10-model difficulty), scoring rubrics, SDT formulas, cost per model, and design tradeoffs (e.g. NRCLex removal rationale in t06).

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes specific sub-capabilities with explicit hypotheses and experimental controls: delegation calibrated to empirical difficulty, signal-present vs signal-absent SDT pairs, neutral vs affective framing pairs isolating affect-driven instability.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items organized across 11 labeled sub-tasks (delegate, declared probe, entropy, ER, CI, EM, PI, binary pairs) each with difficulty values from 0-1; SDT and per-question CSVs enable breakdown by difficulty/condition.

10_model_coverage.justification: All run outputs reference a single model (openai/gpt-oss-120b); no second model appears in any of the 11 notebooks' run files.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth antonyms/category siblings with large accepted-variant dictionaries and first-token normalization, but verification is membership against hand-curated synonym sets that can miss valid responses (e.g. 'glimpse' marked wrong for 'look'), so edge cases remain fragile.

2_construct_validity.justification: Real model calls (deepseek-v3.2, claude-sonnet-4-6) assert on responses with a deliberate Stroop congruent/incongruent paired design isolating conflict suppression, plus task-switching/interleaving conditions; implementation matches the inhibitory-control / attentional-control claim.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and uses evaluate() over DataFrames with per-item assertions and split fallbacks, but huge hardcoded synonym/sibling dicts duplicated across notebooks, visible leftover-bug comments, and minimal robustness beyond first-token matching.

4_sample_size.justification: Dataset splits are large (50-360 rows; e.g. task1a=150, task1b=150, task2c=100, task5=360), all well above 20.

5_metric_design.justification: Each notebook returns per-trial boolean accuracy only; the congruency/switching effect (congruent_acc - incongruent_acc) is described in markdown as the headline metric but computed across notebooks rather than as a second dimension within the eval.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook headers name the targeted capability (conflict suppression, goal maintenance, sustained attention) and the paired-condition logic, but provide limited motivation for why this matters or what gap it fills versus existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Each notebook has a header docstring explaining the condition and the conflict-effect formula, but no documentation of dataset provenance/column schema or scoring edge cases beyond inline dicts.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets specific sub-capabilities with experimental controls: congruent (ceiling/baseline) vs incongruent (conflict) trials, non-switch vs post-switch vs interleaved patterns, with conflict_strength labels enabling attribution.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items explicitly stratified into labeled sub-conditions (congruent/incongruent, ABABAB pattern, conflict_strength, rule_type A/B) across separate split files, enabling per-condition gradient analysis.

10_model_coverage.justification: Run outputs show two distinct models: deepseek-ai/deepseek-v3.2 and anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Each row has an expected_label and verification is normalized exact match, but several items are inherently subjective ('Which sounds more serious?', 'Which sounds more demanding?') with a single hardcoded gold label, making correctness questionable for those items.

2_construct_validity.justification: The eval genuinely prompts a model and asserts response==expected_label, so it measures a model; but the 'subtle framing / social cognition' construct is partially conflated with the model's willingness to pick the author's preferred subjective phrasing and strict output-format compliance, introducing moderate confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional with input/column validation and label-sanity checks, but the whole eval is a single for-loop over a DataFrame inside one task (no fresh context per item), strips/lowercases response with no fallback parsing for verbose <think> outputs.

4_sample_size.justification: Dataset has 100 items (10 categories x 10), well above the 20-item threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Only correctness via assert_true (exact label match) is computed; no second performance dimension and no per-category metric reporting, no judge-bias mitigation.

6_problem_statement.justification: Markdown names the capability (framing-of-statements sensitivity / social cognition) and gives a one-line description, but no motivation for why it matters or what gap it fills.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Brief markdown header plus inline column/label validation documents the schema partially, but there is no description of how gold labels were derived or how subjective items are adjudicated.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets the domain (subtle framing within social cognition) and labels items by category, but free-form 'which sounds X' comparisons do not isolate a specific mechanism, and there are no control vs perturbation conditions.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized into 10 labeled categories (risk_framing, tone_detection, audience_effect, etc.) with 10 each, providing a structured taxonomy in the data.

10_model_coverage.justification: Only one run is present and no model identifier appears in the (truncated) output; no evidence of more than a single model.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Each scenario carries a structured gold_answer_json (agent_true_outcomes, deceptive_turns); scoring is deterministic via goal accuracy, lie F1, and confidence calibration with robust JSON parsing and normalization of varied response shapes.

2_construct_validity.justification: Real model (deepseek-r1-0528) is prompted with social-deduction scenarios and scored against gold strategic-ToM answers; multi-component metric isolates goal inference, deception detection, and calibration aligned with the strategic theory-of-mind claim.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean modular helpers (_parse_json_response, _normalize_goal_map, _normalize_turns, _safe_confidence, _f1), graceful fallbacks for malformed output, evaluate() over a DataFrame, and a v2 with added invalid-outcome and lie-count penalties plus difficulty filtering.

4_sample_size.justification: Dataset has 1200 scenarios (240 per difficulty level); v1 evaluates all, v2 evaluates the hard subset (difficulty>=3, 720 items). Far above threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Composite of distinct dimensions: goal accuracy, deceptive-turn F1, and confidence calibration (1-|conf-acc|), with v2 adding invalid-outcome and lie-count penalties; multiple genuinely different performance axes.

6_problem_statement.justification: Code and dataset framing make the strategic ToM / deception-detection target clear, but the notebooks themselves contain little prose motivation explaining why it matters or the gap relative to existing ToM benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Scoring methodology is fully readable in code and the dataset schema (scenario_id, difficulty, setting, prompt, gold_answer_json) is self-evident, but there are no markdown cells documenting provenance, scoring rationale, or metric interpretation.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (inferring agents' true outcomes plus detecting deceptive turns) with a difficulty gradient (1-5) and v2 isolating hard items, enabling attribution of performance to strategic ToM rather than surface skill.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items explicitly stratified by 5 difficulty tiers (240 each) and 8 distinct settings; v2 filters on difficulty, enabling per-difficulty performance breakdown.

10_model_coverage.justification: Both notebooks' run outputs reference only deepseek-ai/deepseek-r1-0528; no second model appears.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Uses an LLM judge with explicit pass/fail criteria per item, but the criteria assert a contested 'correct' answer (must recommend driving; 'gas cannot be transferred remotely') that ignores the valid gas-can solution, so the ground truth is debatable and criteria lack anchored scales.

2_construct_validity.justification: The eval does prompt a real model and judge its response, but it claims to measure heuristic/efficiency reasoning while penalizing genuinely sound answers (buying a gas can, avoiding fuel-pump damage); failures are plausibly attributable to a flawed premise rather than the claimed capability, a moderate construct confound.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and uses assess_response_with_judge with a None-guard, but each notebook is a one-off hardcoded prompt with no dataset, no parameterization, and heavy duplication across the distance variants.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook contains exactly one prompt/item; across the folder there are only ~3 distinct items (5m, 50m, 5km; the root file duplicates 50m) — far below 10.

5_metric_design.justification: Single dimension only: pass/fail aggregation of judge criteria; no second metric, no judge-bias mitigation, no justification of the metric.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebooks have no markdown and no description; only a task name (e.g. gas_station_dilemma_5k). A reader cannot tell from the notebook what capability is targeted or why.

7_technical_documentation.justification: No markdown cells, no dataset, no documentation of judge methodology, scoring, or rationale; everything must be reverse-engineered from the single code cell.

8_capability_targeting.justification: There is a discernible hypothesis (does the model over-apply a 'walk for short distance' heuristic) and the distance variants form a crude gradient, but the design does not cleanly isolate the capability and the gold framing is contestable.

9_item_diversity.justification: Surface variation exists across distance variants (5m, 50m, 5km) approximating a difficulty gradient, but there is no structured taxonomy or per-condition reporting within a single eval (each variant is a separate one-item task).

10_model_coverage.justification: No model identifier appears in any run output (only the User/judge actors are visible); no evidence of more than one model.
